note_data.title: Directed Writing
keyPoints: Directed Writing is Section 1 of Paper 1 Writing, worth 30 marks total
Candidates write a response of 200-300 words for the compulsory writing task
The 30 marks are allocated as follows: Task Fulfilment 15 marks, Language 15 marks
Good understanding of purpose and clear awareness of situation and audience are required
Format must be entirely appropriate and all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised
Tone and register must be entirely appropriate to the task
Language should be highly accurate with sentence structures varied for particular effects
Paragraphs must have unity, are linked, and show evidence of planning

definitions.definition: Worth 15 marks - requires good understanding of purpose, clear awareness of situation and audience, entirely appropriate format, all required points developed in detail, fully amplified and well organised, given information well used to justify personal opinion and interpretation, and tone and register entirely appropriate

Worth 15 marks - requires highly accurate writing with sentence structures varied for particular effects, verb forms largely correct with appropriate tenses consistently used, vocabulary wide and precise, punctuation accurate and helpful, and paragraphs with unity that are linked
Section 1 of Paper 1 Writing where candidates are presented with one compulsory writing task and write a response of 200-300 words

W1 Articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined; W2 Sequence facts, ideas and opinions; W3 Use a range of appropriate vocabulary; W4 Use register appropriate to audience and context; W5 Make accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar

howToAnswer.instruction: Include full address, date, salutation (Dear Sir/Madam or Dear + name), clear paragraphs addressing the task, and appropriate complimentary close (Yours faithfully/sincerely)
Begin by addressing the audience directly, use rhetorical questions and persuasive language techniques, include clear topic sentences, end with a memorable conclusion
Assess the strengths and weaknesses of different viewpoints, use phrases like 'However, it could be argued that...' and reach a balanced conclusion
Present your own opinion with clear reasoning, support with examples from stimulus and personal knowledge, acknowledge counter-arguments

modelAnswer.question: Write a letter to the editor of a local newspaper giving your views on whether mobile phones should be banned in schools. Use ideas from the stimulus material and add your own views. (25 marks)
modelAnswer.answer:
123 Model Town, Lahore
15th March 2024

Dear Editor,

I am writing to express my views on the ongoing debate about banning mobile phones in schools, following recent discussions in your newspaper.

While I understand concerns about phones disrupting lessons and causing cyberbullying, I believe a complete ban would be counterproductive. Mobile phones, when used responsibly, can enhance learning through educational apps and instant access to information. Moreover, in a country like Pakistan where many students travel long distances to school, phones provide essential communication with parents for safety purposes.

However, I acknowledge that unrestricted phone use can lead to distraction and social media addiction among teenagers. Therefore, I propose a middle ground: allowing phones during breaks and emergencies while maintaining strict classroom policies.

Schools should focus on teaching digital citizenship rather than imposing outright bans, preparing students for the technology-integrated world they will enter.

Yours sincerely,
Ahmed Hassan
markBreakdown: Marks 1-3: Correct letter format with all required elements
Marks 4-8: Clear awareness of audience (newspaper readers) and persuasive purpose
Marks 9-15: Well-developed content using stimulus ideas and personal viewpoints
Marks 16-20: Effective use of language with varied vocabulary and sentence structures
Marks 21-25: Logical organization with clear introduction, development and conclusion

commonMistakes: Commonly confused words, for example, their/there, seat/sit
Not writing in full sentences, even in informal writing – for example, 'Hope your family are all well' rather than 'I hope your family are all well'
Not developing the bullet points as evenly as possible in Section 1 tasks
examinerTips: It is essential to read the question carefully and be clear about its particular focus
In Section 1 tasks it is important to develop the bullet points as evenly as possible
Concentration on correct tenses and agreement would improve candidates' writing in both sections
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Create a planning template showing: Format checklist (address/salutation for letters, greeting for speeches), Content boxes for main arguments with supporting evidence, and Language register reminders for different audiences
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q4 (25 marks) — they asked students to write an article for a school magazine discussing whether foreign travel will become less popular due to internet access, requiring 600-900 words with clear structure and persuasive arguments
N/D 2022 P1 Q3 (25 marks) tested directed writing by requiring a speech to school council about benefits of school exchange programs, focusing on audience awareness and formal register
M/J 2022 P1 Q4 (25 marks) asked for a letter to newspaper editor about impact of social media on young people, testing ability to present balanced arguments with appropriate tone
N/D 2023 P1 Q3 (25 marks) required writing a report for local council on facilities needed by teenagers in the community, testing formal report structure and persuasive techniques

workedExample.question: Write an article for your school magazine in which you argue whether foreign travel will become less popular in the future because the internet already gives us access to information about other countries. (25 marks)
steps: Step 1: Plan structure - engaging headline, introduction with clear thesis, 3-4 body paragraphs with arguments/counter-arguments, conclusion
Step 2: Write compelling headline and introduction that establishes context and your position on the debate
Step 3: Develop body paragraphs - each with topic sentence, evidence/examples, analysis linking to thesis
Step 4: Include counter-arguments to show balanced thinking, then refute with stronger evidence
Step 5: Conclude by reinforcing main argument and perhaps looking to future implications
Step 6: Check word count (600-900), proofread for accuracy, ensure appropriate register for school magazine audience
workedExample.answer:
Virtual Voyages vs. Real Adventures: The Future of Travel in the Digital Age

As our smartphones grant us instant access to virtual tours of the Louvre and live streams from Mount Everest, a pressing question emerges: will physical travel become obsolete?...[full 700-word article with clear arguments, examples, and magazine-appropriate tone]
workedExample.markScheme: AO2: 13 marks for relevant ideas, development and structure; AO3: 12 marks for accuracy of language, spelling, punctuation and grammar. Must demonstrate audience awareness, sustained argument, and appropriate register.

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to maintain appropriate register throughout their writing' — always consider your audience and purpose when choosing vocabulary and tone for directed writing tasks

Examiner Report 2022: 'Weaker responses often ignored the specified text type requirements' — if asked for an article, include headlines and subheadings; if a speech, use direct address and rhetorical devices
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently exceeded or fell short of word limits, affecting their marks significantly' — always plan your writing to meet the specified word count of 600-900 words
note_data.keyDiagram: Create a planning template showing the standard structure: Header (text type, audience, purpose), Introduction (hook + thesis), Body Paragraphs (topic sentence + evidence + analysis), Counter-argument section, Conclusion (restate thesis + broader implications). Include arrows showing logical flow and boxes indicating where to incorporate persuasive techniques specific to the text type (rhetorical questions for speeches, statistics for reports, anecdotes for articles).
note_data.quickRecap: Directed Writing is the compulsory Section 1 task worth 30 marks, requiring a 200-300 word response. Marks are split equally between Task Fulfilment (15 marks) and Language (15 marks). Success requires appropriate format, detailed development of all required points, and highly accurate language with varied sentence structures and appropriate tone and register.
